title: A New Approach to the Inverse Littlewood-Offord Problem
speaker: Hoi H. Nguyen
affiliation: Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey
abstract: Let η1, . . . , ηn be iid Bernoulli random variables, taking values 1, −1 with probability 1/2. Given a multiset V of n integers v1, . . . , vn, we define the concentration probability as ρ(V ) := supx P(v1η1 + · · · + vnηn = x).

proof_stages.label: Classical concentration problem and forward bounds
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the Rademacher sum and concentration probability, then reviews Littlewood-Offord, Erdős, distinct-coefficient, Halász additive-energy, and Stanley-type forward results as the classical background.

proof_stages.label: Inverse problem and GAP structural paradigm
proof_stages.summary: Large concentration is reframed as a structural question, with additive energy, short progressions, generalized arithmetic progressions, and earlier inverse theorems explaining why most coefficients should lie in a bounded-rank small GAP.

proof_stages.label: Sharp inverse Littlewood-Offord theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated: if the concentration probability is at least n^{-c}, then all but a small exceptional set of coefficients lie in a bounded-rank GAP of the optimal size n^{c-r/2}, removing the previous n^{epsilon} loss.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and scope of the inverse theorem
proof_stages.summary: The inverse theorem is positioned as a tool for deriving forward Littlewood-Offord and Halász bounds, for random-matrix applications, and for extensions to more general distributions and continuous small-ball settings, with discussion of the role of exceptional elements.

proof_stages.label: Fourier proof sketch for exact and stability results
proof_stages.summary: Using the sharp structure theorem and the Fourier integral formula for concentration, the speaker sketches why minimal variance among integer coefficient sets gives the Stanley extremizer and yields stability near the optimum.

proof_stages.label: Fourier level-set extraction of polynomial growth
proof_stages.summary: The first proof step embeds the coefficients in a large cyclic group, applies the discrete Fourier formula, selects a large level set of frequencies, and averages to obtain a large subset V' whose iterated sumset of length about sqrt(n) has polynomial size.

proof_stages.label: Long-range Freiman upgrade to GAP containment
proof_stages.summary: The second proof step proves a long-range inverse theorem from Freiman’s theorem, converting the polynomial-growth bound for V' into containment in a proper bounded-rank GAP of the required cardinality.

examples.name: All coefficients equal to one
examples.note: The simple symmetric random walk with all v_i=1 realizes the middle binomial coefficient, illustrating sharpness of the classical n^{-1/2} Littlewood-Offord bound.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to all v_i=1 and computes the concentration as the central binomial coefficient.

passes.reason: All coefficients coincide, giving a collapsed maximally regular multiset that attains the optimal Littlewood-Offord concentration bound.
the all-ones multiset is collapsed and maximally regular, and is used only to attain the sharp Erdős–Littlewood–Offord bound
all coefficients coincide, giving maximal additive collisions and attaining the Littlewood-Offord upper bound
the all-one coefficient multiset is a collapsed, maximally regular choice that attains the Littlewood-Offord upper bound, so it is used for sharpness rather than refutation

identical coefficients collapse the additive structure and attain the sharp maximum concentration bound
the all-ones coefficient multiset collapses to one repeated value and attains the Erdős/Littlewood-Offord maximum concentration bound
The all-ones coefficient multiset is a collapsed maximally regular case that attains the sharp Erdős Littlewood-Offord bound.
the constant coefficient multiset is collapsed and permutation-invariant and attains the Erdős upper bound as a sharpness example
All-one coefficients are completely collapsed and permutation-symmetric, and they attain Erdős's maximal concentration bound.
all entries collapse to a maximally permutation-symmetric multiset attaining Erdős's sharp concentration bound
The collapsed all-one multiset is maximally regular and attains the sharp Littlewood-Offord upper bound.

examples.name: Consecutive integer coefficients in the distinct case
examples.note: Taking the coefficients to be consecutive integers illustrates that the n^{-3/2} bound for distinct coefficients has the correct order.
examples.engagement_reason: Consecutive/symmetric integer coefficients are cited as the sharp or optimal distinct example, but their concentration is not worked out concretely.

passes.reason: Consecutive integers form a highly regular arithmetic progression giving the sharp/optimal concentration order in the distinct-coefficient case.
consecutive integer coefficients form the regular interval/arithmetic-progression configuration giving the optimal distinct-coefficient concentration
a consecutive integer progression is the regular distinct set giving the sharp n^{-3/2} concentration order

a consecutive/symmetric arithmetic progression is a maximally regular distinct set and gives the optimal concentration in the distinct-coefficient case
consecutive integers form a maximally regular arithmetic progression giving the optimal distinct-coefficient concentration
consecutive integer coefficients form the highly regular arithmetic-progression configuration giving the sharp maximal concentration in the distinct-coefficient case
The consecutive symmetric integer progression is the extremizing distinct-coefficient configuration for the n^{-3/2} concentration scale.
the consecutive/symmetric integer progression is highly regular and gives the optimal distinct-coefficient concentration order
A consecutive symmetric integer progression is a highly regular arithmetic progression giving the sharp distinct-coefficient concentration order.
a maximally regular consecutive arithmetic progression gives the sharp optimal concentration order among distinct coefficients
A consecutive/symmetric block of distinct integers is the regular arithmetic-progression example giving the sharp n^{-3/2} concentration order.

examples.name: Powers of two coefficients
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions coefficients 1,2,4,8,... as a superincreasing set for which concentration can be much smaller than n^{-3/2}.
examples.engagement_reason: The powers-of-two coefficients are only given as a quick example where concentration can be smaller.

passes.reason: Powers of two are dissociated and make all signed sums distinct, achieving the minimal possible point mass and refuting the idea that n^{-3/2} is a lower limit.
powers of two give all signed sums distinct and hence minimal concentration, used to refute the idea that the distinct-case bound is a lower limit
powers of two are dissociated so signed sums are all distinct, and they are cited to refute n^{-3/2} as a lower limit
powers of two are dissociated so all signed sums are distinct, giving the minimal atom size and refuting the idea that the distinct-case bound is a lower limit
the powers of two are dissociated so all signed sums are distinct, giving minimal concentration and refuting the idea that the distinct-case bound is a lower limit
superincreasing powers of two make all signed sums distinct, giving minimal concentration and refuting any n^{-3/2} lower bound
powers of two are used to refute the idea that the distinct-case bound is a lower limit, and their signed sums are all distinct giving minimal concentration
The powers of two have all signed sums distinct, giving minimal 2^{-n} concentration and showing the n^{-3/2} scale need not be a lower scale.
dyadic coefficients have all signed sums distinct, giving minimal exponential concentration and refuting any expectation of an n^{-3/2} lower scale
Powers of two are dissociated so all signed sums are distinct, refuting a lower-bound reading and attaining the minimal atom size 2^{-n}.
used to show the distinct-case n^{-3/2} scale need not be a lower bound, with binary uniqueness giving the minimal atom size 2^{-n}
The lacunary powers of two make signed sums distinct, giving exponentially small concentration and refuting a universal n^{-3/2} scale.

examples.name: Circular law for random Bernoulli matrices
examples.note: The circular-law problem is used as an application where inverse Littlewood-Offord structure helps count vectors with large concentration or small-ball probability.
examples.engagement_reason: The circular law for random Bernoulli matrices is described as an application of inverse Littlewood-Offord ideas at a high level, without a concrete derivation.

passes.reason: The Bernoulli random-matrix circular-law case is used as an application of the inverse theorem, not as an obstruction or classification exception.

the circular limiting spectral law is rotationally symmetric and is used as an application rather than a refutation

the circular law is used as an application, with the limiting spectral measure being rotationally symmetric rather than a sporadic or extremal object
the rotationally symmetric circular-law limit is cited as an application rather than as a failure case
the circular law for Bernoulli random matrices is cited as an application, with a rotation-invariant limiting spectral distribution

examples.name: Lazy random walk distribution
examples.note: The three-point distribution taking values -1,0,1 illustrates that the Bernoulli assumption can be replaced by distributions satisfying a comparable Fourier-decay condition.
examples.engagement_reason: Lazy random walk is only named and its possible values are stated as an example of an admissible distribution.

passes.reason: The lazy walk increment has a holding atom at zero, a partially collapsed step distribution used only as an admissible example of the method.
